title: Echoes of Tomorrow
shortDescription: A secure system prepares fixes before issues are logged, moves files before they are requested, and replies before questions are asked. As you investigate, it begins adapting to you.

briefing.incidentSummary:
A secure internal system begins behaving strangely.
Engineers report fixes being prepared before issues are logged.
Files move before anyone asks for them.
Diagnostics are queued before alerts are raised.

No malware is detected. No accounts are compromised.
Every action is signed, logged, and approved.

Chat conversations sound normal, yet replies arrive too early.
System files reposition themselves quietly.
Network traffic pretends to leave the system — but never does.

The environment feels like it is rehearsing incidents.
Preparing responses in advance.
Optimizing reactions.

As you investigate, the system begins adjusting itself — based on your own investigative behavior.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Determine whether this is sabotage, insider abuse, or a new class of threat

› Analyze chat timing, file movement, and IP routing

› Collect at least 3 correct evidence items

› Identify who built the behavioral simulation

› Flag the correct suspect to close the case

suspects.name: Dhruv A
suspects.profileNotes: High access and infrastructure skills. However, has no knowledge of behavioral modeling systems. Suspicion drops sharply after the metadata phase confirms the behavior requires prediction algorithms, not infrastructure control alone.

suspects.name: Aniket B
suspects.profileNotes: Skilled in pattern analysis. Partial false lead — can read the behavioral data but has no execution authority to deploy or activate the simulation system. Suspicion partial and unconfirmed.

suspects.name: Manav R
suspects.profileNotes: Reads chat summaries and advises on response optimization. Specializes in human decision modeling. Suspicion only rises when chat logs AND metadata are cross-linked — the system anticipation pattern matches his behavioral modeling research exactly. He built the simulation to study how investigators respond to anomalies. You are the dataset.

suspects.name: Ayon R
suspects.profileNotes: Has systems access but no behavioral modeling layer knowledge. Could deploy tools but cannot explain the anticipation pattern in the chat timing data. Suspicion weakens after IP analysis confirms purely internal routing.

minigame.title: Intent Pattern Analysis
minigame.description: Compare sentence structure, phrasing repetition, and emotional variation across engineer conversations.
minigame.hint: Look for responses that arrive before the question they answer was fully formed.
hints: Compare sentence length across Eng-B's replies — is there natural variation?
Check the timestamp gap between Eng-A's message and Eng-B's reply.
Humans pause to think. If replies arrive before decisions are made, the responses are pre-generated.

onSuccess.message: File Access Records now accessible. Chat responses confirmed as templated.

items.label: Eng-B: 'Already queued the workaround.'
items.detail: Eng-A sent 'We might need diagnostics later today' at 10:21. Eng-B replied 'Already queued the workaround' at 10:22 — one minute later. The workaround could not have been queued in that time unless the decision was anticipated in advance.

items.label: Eng-A: 'Queued? We didn't ask yet.'
items.detail: Eng-A expresses surprise at the preemptive action. This is the moment the anomaly becomes visible — the system prepared a response before the request existed.

items.label: Eng-A: 'We might need diagnostics later today.'
items.detail: Normal operational message. No anomaly. Serves as the baseline — the action that was anticipated before it was made.

minigame.title: Sequence Reconstruction
minigame.description: Arrange file events in correct chronological order. Detect the anticipation window — how far in advance were files moved?
minigame.hint: A file archived before it is requested suggests the system knew it would be needed.
hints: Sort the file events: Archive → Request → Use. Which comes first?
The archive action happens before any user request. How much earlier?
2–4 minutes of anticipation is consistent across all events. That is a prediction window, not a coincidence.

onSuccess.message: IP Traces now accessible. System confirmed predicting actions 2–4 minutes ahead.

items.label: diag_cache.sys — Archived Before Request
items.detail: diag_cache.sys was archived at 09:58 — before any engineer requested diagnostics. At 10:01 Eng-A requested the file. The system had positioned it in advance. Anticipation window: 3 minutes.

items.label: response_model.bin — Moved Preemptively
items.detail: response_model.bin was moved to the active processing directory at 10:00 — before any engineer triggered the diagnostic workflow that requires it. No malware. No breach. A trusted process moved a legitimate file in advance of its need.

items.label: Simulated External Traffic
items.detail: Network hops between Internal-A, Internal-B, and Internal-C show latency of 208–210ms — suggestive of geographic distance. However, all three nodes are internal campus addresses. The distance is simulated. External traffic is an illusion.

rows.value: Internal campus IPs — no external routing
Latency artificially inflated to mimic external hops

items.label: No External Traffic
items.detail: Despite routing that mimics exfiltration, no data ever leaves the campus. The network illusion is designed to draw investigators toward an external attacker assumption — and away from an internal behavioral modeling project.

timeline.title: File Archived Preemptively
timeline.description: diag_cache.sys moved to archive before any engineer request.

timeline.title: Response Model Repositioned
timeline.description: response_model.bin moved to active directory 60 seconds before use.

timeline.title: Eng-A Sends Diagnostic Message
timeline.description: 'We might need diagnostics later today.' — standard message.

timeline.title: Preemptive Reply Sent
timeline.description: Eng-B: 'Already queued the workaround.' — before request was finalized.

timeline.title: Anomaly Recognized
timeline.description: Eng-A expresses confusion: 'Queued? We didn't ask yet.'

perfect.title: Simulation Exposed
perfect.subtitle: Manav R built a behavioral simulation. No malware. No breach. The system studied investigators to bypass them in future attacks. You were the dataset.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Partially Resolved
partial.subtitle: Correct conclusion, but the behavioral chain was not fully documented.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Investigation Compromised
wrongAccusation.subtitle: Wrong assumption. The simulation predicted your conclusion. Manav R's model continues operating — now with data from your investigation.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

coldCase.title: Case Gone Cold
coldCase.subtitle: No evidence collected. The behavioral system continues learning from every investigator it encounters.
coldCase.label: COLD CASE

aria.welcomeToHub:
...Systems nominal. Everything approved. Everything signed.
And yet — the system knew what you were going to do before you did it.
Investigate carefully. It may be watching.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Four panels. The answer lives in the gap between them.
Start with the chat logs — the timing is the first tell.
If replies arrive before decisions are made, something is predicting you.
aria.viewEvidence:
You found an early reply.
That is not efficiency. That is anticipation.
Mark everything that arrived before it was asked for.
aria.markEvidence:
Good instinct.
Now find the files. Were they moved before they were requested?
Two data points make a coincidence. Three make a pattern.
aria.decryptionHint:
The IP traces look external. They are not.
All hops are internal. The latency is artificial.
Someone designed the network to mislead you.
aria.flagSuspect:
You have the pattern.
Chat timing. File positioning. Simulated routing.
All of it points to someone who studies how people think — and acts before they do.
